text: The surgeon demonstrates the placement of laparoscopic ports, positioning the camera lateral to the ostomy, which is visible to the right of the camera view on the screen.
The surgeon carefully dissects the omentum from the anterior surface of the gallbladder, ensuring smooth separation of the tissue.
The surgeon begins the dissection to open the peritoneum at the inferior aspect of the gallbladder.
The surgeon places the laparoscopic ports slightly above the umbilicus in a patient with a high BMI, adjusting the positioning for optimal access.
The cystic duct is clearly visible in the surgical field, allowing for precise identification and subsequent dissection.
The surgeon creates a window above the artery, which is positioned directly on top of the duct, and begins separating the gallbladder from the liver bed to establish the critical view.
The surgeon releases more of the posterior aspect of the gallbladder from the liver bed, further separating the two structures.
The surgeon achieves a clear critical view of the surgical field, where the cystic duct and artery are the only visible structures connecting the gallbladder to the liver.
The surgeon applies clips to the cystic duct, securing it as part of the cholestectomy procedure.
The surgeon clips the artery and proceeds to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed. The gallbladder appears thickened and inflamed, but the detachment process is manageable.
The surgeon lifts the gallbladder off the liver bed, causing minor bleeding due to tearing of the liver capsule.
The surgeon inspects the liver bed, achieving hemostasis. The ducts are clearly visible, including the bifurcation of the common duct into the hepatic ducts.
